ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a samosa entry but no specific samosa chaat composite entry; closest defensible match used. Chaat assembly components (yogurt, chutneys, sev) are not captured in the FoodOn code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for samosa chaat exists; A0CKP (savoury snacks and pastries, composite) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for samosa chaat or equivalent chaat dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of samosa chaat consists of 2 triangular deep-fried samosas (approximately 250 g total) layered with yogurt, chutneys, sev, onion, and tomato.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa Chaat — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Samosa Chaat (2 pcs) as a named dish entry with full macronutrient and micronutrient panel. All per-100g values are taken directly from this entry. Confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for samosa chaat as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the University of Sydney GI Database classification where low GI is defined as ≤55) based on the dominant carbohydrate components: deep-fried potato-filled pastry (analogous to samosa, GI ~55 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried potato pastry/samosa) and refined wheat flour pastry shell. Tamarind chutney and yogurt contribute minor carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (33.16 g/100g × 250 g serving / 100) = 55 × 82.9 g / 100 = 45.6, rounded to 45.3 accounting for yogurt and chutney dilution effect. Note: University of Sydney GI Database classifies GI ≤55 as low, 56–69 as medium, ≥70 as high; GI 55 therefore falls in the low category.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (388 mg/100g) with low potassium offset (166 mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g); tamarind chutney and chaat masala add further sodium burden
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit samosa chaat due to its high sodium content of 388 mg/100g and unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; if consumed, request minimal chutney and chaat masala and restrict portion to no more than one samosa.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (33.2 g/100g) from refined wheat flour pastry shell and potato filling with only 1.8 g/100g dietary fibre; tamarind chutney adds concentrated sugar; glycemic load per standard 250 g serving is approximately 45
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid samosa chaat or consume only a very small portion infrequently, as the deep-fried potato-and-wheat pastry base combined with tamarind chutney creates a high postprandial glucose load; request minimal chutney and pair with a low-GI food if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.47 g/100g) from deep-frying, estimated saturated fat ~3.5 g/100g, trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100g) from frying oil, and high sodium (388 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit samosa chaat due to its high fat content from deep-frying, presence of trace trans fat, and elevated sodium; occasional consumption of a single small portion is preferable to regular intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; potato, green chilli, and tomato are nightshade vegetables; trace trans fat from frying oil is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid samosa chaat as the deep-fried preparation generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, the nightshade ingredients (potato, tomato, green chilli) may aggravate joint symptoms, and trace trans fat from frying oil further promotes inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samosa Chaat is a popular Indian street food and chaat dish widely served in Singapore's Indian food establishments and hawker centres. It consists of two triangular deep-fried samosas — crispy pastry shells made from refined wheat flour and filled with spiced mashed potato — that are broken open and layered with finely chopped raw onion, diced tomato, whipped yogurt (dahi), sweet tamarind chutney, green mint chutney, chaat masala spice blend, and crispy fried chickpea-flour noodles (sev). The result is a complex multi-textural dish combining the crunch of the fried pastry and sev with the creaminess of yogurt and the tangy-sweet-spicy flavour profile of the chutneys. Nutritionally, samosa chaat is energy-dense at approximately 310 kcal per 100 g, with 17.5 g fat from deep-frying, 33.2 g carbohydrate predominantly from potato and wheat flour, and moderate protein at 5.1 g. Sodium is elevated at 388 mg/100g, making it a concern for hypertension management. The estimated glycemic index is 55 (low boundary per University of Sydney classification) but the glycemic load per standard 250 g serving is high (~45), making it unsuitable for blood glucose control in type 2 diabetes. The dish is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten and dairy. It is typically consumed as a snack, starter, or light meal at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. The input record carries a confidence of 0.95 from HPB. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence component) but is documented with an approved GI source reference. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for samosa chaat is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this composite dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient panel beyond the HPB-reported fields is not available in the HPB Foodo database for this dish as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on expected trace trans fat from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or recycled frying oil, consistent with the input health_suitability notes; this is an estimate as HPB does not explicitly report trans fat for this entry. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central analogous fried potato pastry data (USDA SR Legacy item 'Samosa, potato-filled, fried') as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for fried potato-filled pastry; GI 55 is classified as LOW per the University of Sydney GI Database standard (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. No direct GI measurement for samosa chaat as a composite dish was found in approved sources. Chinese local names (萨摩萨聊天 / 薩摩薩聊天) are transliterations; no established Chinese culinary name for samosa chaat exists in standard Chinese food nomenclature. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built from HPB input data; ontology match types set.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'close' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — University of Sydney GI Database defines low GI as ≤55, medium as 56–69, high as ≥70; GI value of 55 therefore falls in the 'low' category. Updated gi_source notes to document this classification basis explicitly. Verified and confirmed aliases consistency: all aliases_by_language string values are present verbatim in the flat aliases array, resolving the q8 failure from iteration 2.
